text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic extended pelvic lymphadenectomy, incising the pelvic peritoneum over the external iliac vessels between the infundibular pelvic and round ligaments. The retroperitoneal space is entered, and dissection proceeds along the umbilical artery and fascia. The round ligament is coagulated and cut, revealing the obliterated umbilical artery.
The surgeon retracts the umbilical artery medially, revealing the medial border of the dissection. The vesico-umbilical fascia is penetrated, and the LASCO space is entered from the lateral side.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the visceral branches of the internal iliac vessels and the lymph node-bearing tissue within the lateral parameter. The dissection progresses dorsally, exposing the pelvic floor and the arcus tendineus of the levator ani muscle. Remaining small visceral branches of the internal iliac vessels are coagulated and cut, while the urethra is identified medially to the internal iliac artery and retracted away from the dissection field.

The surgeon frees the proximal internal iliac artery from surrounding lymph node-bearing tissue, visualizing the root of the inferior gluteal artery. The dorsal branches of the internal iliac vessels are coagulated and cut up to the pelvic floor, fully mobilizing and lateralizing the lateral parametrium.

The surgeon dissects along the psoas and obturator fascia, detaching the external iliac artery and vein from the obturator fascia along with surrounding lymph node-bearing tissue. The iliosacral joint is identified, and dissection continues caudally and dorsally along the internus obturator fascia. Small parietal vessels are coagulated and cut, ensuring no tissue remains on the fascia surface.

The surgeon halts dissection upon reaching the obturator vessels, then proceeds along the endopelvic sciatic nerve. The common iliac vessels are retracted medially, exposing the iliolumbar fossa where the iliolumbar vessels are identified. The iliolumbar vein is coagulated and cut to create more space for dissection. The lumbosacral trunk is identified and followed to establish the dissection plane over the sciatic plexus. Small vessels exiting the pelvis over the sciatic notch are coagulated and cut. Lymph node-bearing tissue and the vascular web over the sciatic plexus are gradually detached and moved medially.

The surgeon encounters the inferior gluteal artery and cleans the obturator nerve of surrounding tissue. The superior gluteal artery is identified and preserved. Dissection proceeds along the external iliac vessels, which are cleaned from the bifurcation up to the inguinal canal in a traditional manner.

The surgeon identifies and sacrifices the pubic vein using a hemolog clip. Lymphatics are detached from the pubic bone, and the obturator vein is coagulated and cut. Partial resection of the anterior division of the internal iliac vessels is performed. The pelvic lymph nodes and the vascular web of the internal iliac vessels are detached from the pelvic fascia and sciatic nerve, then medialized. The obturator artery is cut at the entrance to the obturator canal.

The surgeon visualizes the distal inferior gluteal artery and pudendal artery. The parietal and visceral branches of the internal iliac vessels are coagulated and cut at their origin, removing all lymph node-bearing tissue and incorporated vessels in one block. The remaining lymph nodes are excised from the iliolumbar fossa. The final view shows the obturator muscle, levator ani muscle, coccygeus muscle, and sciatic nerve plexus completely clean of lymphatic and fatty tissue.
